output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does scoring a synthetic tabular data generator's causal faithfulness with a single graph-averaged scalar (SHD, F1, precision, recall, or 100xAMAE) computed from ONE fixed causal-discovery readout - a fixed algorithm such as PC or DirectLiNGAM, a fixed conditional-independence test, and a fixed conditioning-set sampler - conflate generator-induced causal distortion with readout-estimator error and average away WHERE structure is lost, so that it must be promoted to an estimator-marginalized, per-claim-and-level causal-fidelity profile that separates the generator-attributable discrepancy (stable across readouts) from the readout-attributable discrepancy (varying with the estimator) and provably collapses back to the original scalar when the estimator ensemble is a single fixed algorithm and the per-claim weights are uniform?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Synthetic tabular data is no longer just a privacy or augmentation convenience - it is becoming a SUBSTRATE for downstream CAUSAL inference: treatment-effect estimation, policy evaluation, counterfactual and fairness analyses run on synthetic stand-ins for sensitive records. If a generator silently distorts causal structure (flips an edge, mutes an intervention), every downstream causal conclusion drawn on that data is wrong, and the error is invisible to the Kolmogorov-Smirnov / correlation / Train-on-Synthetic-Test-on-Real / Distance-to-Closest-Record checks that currently gate release. Worse, the source benchmark's single fixed readout cannot say whether a low score is the GENERATOR'S fault or the READOUT'S fault; that ambiguity mis-ranks generators and makes guidance NON-REPRODUCIBLE across labs that use different causal-discovery tools (PC vs GES vs LiNGAM, Fisher-Z vs kernel CI) - two groups can rank the same generators in opposite orders purely from readout choice.

The promotion converts "rank generators by one number" into "AUDIT generators with an estimator-robust, localized causal-fidelity PROFILE plus attribution," yielding three durable artifacts: (i) for generator DEVELOPERS, a DIAGNOSTIC - which specific edges, orientations, mechanisms, or density regions to fix; (ii) for downstream USERS, a CERTIFICATE - which causal queries are safe to run on this synthetic dataset and which are not; (iii) for the field, REPRODUCIBLE cross-lab rankings, because the generator-attributable component is reported separately from readout variance. The estimator-marginalization + readout-swap-Placebo recipe is also a TEMPLATE: any generative-model evaluation whose readout is itself a learned or estimated probe - Frechet Inception Distance with its Inception extractor, LLM-as-judge scoring, learned perceptual metrics - has the same disease and can adopt the same measurement-hygiene cure.

Concretely it advances: scientific data sharing (release synthetic cohorts with a causal certificate so re-analysts know which mechanistic questions remain answerable), healthcare and econometrics (prevent drawing a causal conclusion on an edge the generator never preserved), AutoML and augmentation (select generators by causal fidelity where it matters, not just marginal realism), and causal-benchmark standardization (certify that a benchmark measures the generator, not its own estimator, raising the bar for what counts as a trustworthy generative-evaluation result).

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

Throughout, a CLAIM is an individual causal assertion - a skeleton edge, an edge orientation, a d-separation triple (Xi independent of Yi given Si), an interventional query, or a counterfactual query - and a LEVEL denotes which of these causal-information levels the claim belongs to; claims are further stratified by mechanism type (linear, nonlinear) and edge density (dense hub vs sparse leaf). The crux is ATTRIBUTION (defect i: estimator-entanglement); the payoff is LOCALIZATION (defect ii: isotropy). The obstacles are separable:

1. ATTRIBUTION IDENTIFIABILITY (the central difficulty). A measured discrepancy (e.g., inflated SHD between the real-graph and synthetic-graph readouts) is a CONVOLUTION of two sources: generator distortion of the joint density, and readout-estimator error (finite-sample causal-discovery and CI-test mistakes). Both inflate the SAME scalar, so one number cannot separate them. Why naive fails: "just average over estimators" can MASK a real generator failure when all estimators share a bias - constraint-, score-, and FCM-based methods ALL degrade on dense / high-in-degree graphs, so their consensus error looks like generator fidelity. Why tractable: treat it as a variance-components / two-way-ANOVA (mixed-effects) problem over a DIVERSE estimator ensemble crossed with generators, and CALIBRATE attribution by INJECTING synthetic ground-truth distortions of known magnitude into the structural causal model (SCM), so the generator-attributable component can be regressed against a known signal. Critically, the generator x estimator INTERACTION variance is reported as a third, separately labeled component (the estimator-dependent generator effect); it is not absorbed into either main effect, and a large interaction term is itself a flagged failure of estimator-robustness.

2. ENSEMBLE CANONICALIZATION. Different causal-discovery algorithms return different OBJECTS: skeletons (PC adjacency), Markov-equivalence classes / CPDAGs (GES, FCI with PAGs), and fully-oriented DAGs (DirectLiNGAM, NOTEARS). Why naive fails: averaging SHD across these compares incommensurable outputs (an unoriented edge is not a wrong orientation). Why tractable: project every output onto a COMMON causal-claim vocabulary before comparison - per-edge adjacency (skeleton), per-edge orientation (only where identifiable), per d-separation triple, and per interventional / counterfactual query - so marginalization happens claim-by-claim over a shared schema.

3. FINITE-SAMPLE CI-TEST VARIANCE. d-separation readouts are noisy; the conditioning-set choice biases WHICH claims get tested, and the {(Xi,Yi,Si)} d-connected / d-separated triple sampler is itself a design knob. Why naive fails: a single CI run at one alpha gives brittle, non-reproducible edge verdicts. Why tractable: bootstrap CI tests, aggregate p-values, apply multiple-testing control (Benjamini-Hochberg FDR), and stratify by conditioning-set size; report claim verdicts with uncertainty rather than hard 0/1.

4. GROUND-TRUTH ASYMMETRY. Exact attribution needs a known graph, which exists only on synthetic SCMs. Real data (Beijing air quality) has no gold DAG, and COUNTERFACTUAL fidelity has NO observable ground truth even in real data. Why naive fails: claiming localization accuracy on real data assumes a graph you do not have. Why tractable: do exact attribution / injection calibration on synthetic SCMs; on real data use consensus / known-structure benchmarks (Sachs, bnlearn Asia/Alarm/Child/Hepar) plus sensitivity analysis, and confine counterfactual claims to simulator SCMs where the structural equations are known.

5. COMBINATORIAL COST. estimators x CI-tests x bootstraps x generators x datasets x per-claim is a large grid. Why naive fails: full enumeration is infeasible. Why tractable: claim subsampling (stratified by mechanism / density), lazy / early-stopping evaluation when variance components have converged, and reuse of fitted causal-discovery outputs across claim queries.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

POSITION AGAINST PRIOR ART (by strand):

1) Synthetic tabular-data EVALUATION (statistical / ML-efficiency / privacy). The dominant axes are statistical fidelity (Kolmogorov-Smirnov, total-variation / chi-square on categoricals, pairwise-correlation and correlation-difference matrices), machine-learning efficiency (Train-on-Synthetic-Test-on-Real, TSTR, and its converse), and privacy (Distance-to-Closest-Record, membership-inference attacks). Tooling encodes exactly these: SDV / SDMetrics, table-evaluator, syntheval, and profiling-style reports. KEY GAP: every one treats a row as an i.i.d. draw from a distribution; they certify that P(X) matches, never that the data-generating MECHANISM matches. A generator can ace KS, correlation, and TSTR while inverting an edge or destroying an interventional relationship. Causal structure is simply out of scope for this strand - it stopped short because distribution-matching was the stated contract and was already cheap to compute.

2) The SOURCE benchmark (causal evaluation of tabular generators: TabSyn, STASY, TabDDPM, CoDi, GReaT, CTGAN, TVAE; skeleton via constraint-based causal discovery, directions via LiNGAM, interventional / counterfactual via 100xAMAE; SCM datasets LG/LU/SG/NG + Beijing air quality; "ref." oracle). This is the closest work and the right question - but it scores faithfulness through ONE fixed causal-discovery readout (a chosen algorithm + a chosen CI test) collapsed to a graph-averaged SCALAR (SHD / F1 / AMAE). Its own caveat ("a worse score vs pseudo-labels does not necessarily mean poor performance, only relative differences") IS the admission of the confound: the number entangles generator-induced causal distortion with readout-estimator error and variance, and averages over edges / mechanisms / levels so it cannot localize where structure is lost. That confound is precisely what we remove.

3) Causal-discovery EVALUATION metrics. Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), Structural Intervention Distance (SID; Peters and Buhlmann 2015, Neural Computation), and F1 on skeleton / CPDAG are the standard rulers. But they were built to grade causal-discovery ALGORITHMS against a known graph on FIXED data; nobody applies them as functionals to be MARGINALIZED over the estimator inside a generative benchmark. The ruler was treated as neutral, not as a variance source.

4) Causal-discovery BENCHMARK suites. bnlearn reference networks (Asia, Alarm, Sachs, Hepar2), causal-learn, gCastle / Trustworthy-AI, CausalBench (Chevalley et al. 2023 [to verify]), BEELINE for gene regulatory networks (Pratapa et al. 2020 [to verify]), SynTReN (Van den Bulcke et al. 2006 [to verify]), and the Causal Discovery Toolbox / CDT (Kalainathan and Goudet 2019 [to verify]). All benchmark ESTIMATORS on data; none benchmark GENERATORS, and none decompose generator-vs-readout. The two communities (generative-eval and causal-discovery-eval) are organizationally separate, which is a large reason the cross-product was never built.

5) Estimator STABILITY / uncertainty in causal discovery. Stability selection (Meinshausen and Buhlmann 2010, JRSS-B), Causal Stability Ranking / CStaR (Stekhoven et al. 2012, Bioinformatics [to verify]), bootstrap edge-confidence, and ensemble causal discovery quantify how reproducible an EDGE is under resampling. This is the technical seed we borrow - but it has only ever been used for edge confidence on a single dataset, never to ATTRIBUTE variance between "the generator" and "the readout" in a generative benchmark, and never turned into a per-claim fidelity field.

6) Generative-eval CONFOUND literature (analogy / transfer). The Frechet Inception Distance's dependence on its Inception feature extractor and the critiques that the probe network distorts the score (Kynkaanniemi et al. 2023, ICLR [to verify]); Kernel Inception Distance; LLM-as-judge position / verbosity bias. Same disease - the measurement probe is itself a learned or estimated object that injects its own bias - but diagnosed in image and text generation, never ported to causal-structure readouts on tabular generators, and never with a formal marginalization remedy.

THE UNCLAIMED QUADRANT (what no one has done): (a) treat the causal-discovery READOUT as a CONFOUNDER to be marginalized inside a GENERATOR benchmark (read out under an ensemble of constraint-, score-, and FCM-based methods plus multiple CI tests), rather than fixing one algorithm; (b) use a VARIANCE-COMPONENTS decomposition to split GENERATOR-attributable causal distortion from READOUT-attributable estimator error / variance - an attribution, not just an aggregate; (c) produce a per-CLAIM causal-fidelity FIELD (per-edge skeleton, per-edge orientation, per d-separation triple, per interventional query, per counterfactual query), mechanism- and density-stratified, instead of one scalar; (d) use a readout-swap PLACEBO as a benchmark-VALIDITY test (holding the generator fixed and swapping algorithm / CI test, the scalar generator-RANKING should move while the marginalized ranking stays stable); (e) PROVE the construction reduces to the existing scalars (the FID and LLM-judge cases are positioning analogies, not boundary identities). Each strand stopped short because the scalar was cheap and shippable, per-claim attribution looked combinatorially and statistically intractable (many triples x many estimators x many mechanisms), the causal-discovery-eval and generative-eval communities publish in different venues with different rulers, and, decisively, everyone assumed the causal-discovery readout was a neutral instrument rather than a biased estimator that must be marginalized out.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

UNITS UNDER TEST (fixed, paper set): TabSyn, STASY, TabDDPM, CoDi, GReaT, CTGAN, TVAE.

DATASETS. (a) Synthetic SCMs with KNOWN ground-truth graphs and controllable DISTORTION INJECTION: linear Gaussian (LG), linear non-Gaussian / uniform (LU, LiNGAM-identifiable), nonlinear additive-noise and post-nonlinear (SG/NG), generated over Erdos-Renyi and scale-free (Barabasi-Albert) graphs of varied node count and edge density; injection = controlled perturbations to chosen structural coefficients / added-or-removed edges of known magnitude. (b) Real / benchmark: Beijing air quality (paper); Sachs protein-signaling (consensus graph); bnlearn networks Asia, Alarm, Child, Hepar (known structures).

READOUT ENSEMBLE (estimator-marginalized). Constraint-based: PC, FCI. Score-based: GES. FCM-based: DirectLiNGAM. Continuous-optimization: NOTEARS, GraN-DAG. CI tests: Fisher-Z (linear-Gaussian), KCI and RCIT (nonlinear). Libraries: causal-learn, gCastle, CDT, bnlearn, DoWhy (interventional / counterfactual queries).

METRICS. Existing: SHD, F1, precision, recall on skeleton and CPDAG; SID; 100xAMAE for interventional / counterfactual queries. NEW (the promotion - an estimator-marginalized, per-claim causal-fidelity PROFILE): (i) VARIANCE COMPONENTS - fraction of discrepancy variance explained by GENERATOR vs READOUT-estimator, via two-way ANOVA / mixed-effects over generator x estimator x dataset, with the generator x estimator INTERACTION reported as a separately labeled third component (estimator-dependent generator effect), never folded into either main effect. (ii) RANKING STABILITY under readout swap - Kendall-tau / rank correlation of the generator ranking across estimators, computed for the SCALAR readout vs the PROFILE (the headline disentanglement result). (iii) LOCALIZATION accuracy - on synthetic data with INJECTED known edge distortion, precision / recall of the per-claim fidelity field at flagging the distorted edges / claims vs injected ground truth. (iv) ATTRIBUTION CALIBRATION - regression of the generator-attributable component against injected distortion MAGNITUDE: the component must be monotone increasing with slope bounded away from zero; a slope near 1 after calibration is the target, not a precondition for validity.

BASELINES. Source scalar SHD/F1/AMAE under a single fixed readout; statistical-fidelity metrics (SDMetrics, TSTR train-on-synthetic-test-on-real) as causal-blind baselines; single-readout per-edge map (boundary B3).

EXACT REDUCTIONS [provable-by-construction]. B1 = estimator ensemble {single fixed algorithm} + uniform per-claim weights -> the original graph-averaged scalar (SHD/F1/precision/recall and 100xAMAE). B2 = uniform weights + a single readout -> the graph-averaged SHD. B3 = no estimator-marginalization but claim-resolution kept -> a per-edge fidelity map under one readout (a weaker, already-known diagnostic). B4 = the reference graph passed through the IDENTICAL fixed-readout scalar computation -> the paper's "ref." row; because this is the same function applied to the same input, it reproduces "ref." by construction (bit-exact up to fixed-seed CI-test RNG), an identity rather than an approximate reproduction.

FALSIFIER LADDER (decisive). ORACLE: the B4 identity above - real / reference data through the pipeline reproduces the "ref." row by construction. PLACEBO (readout-swap, the central test): fix the generator, swap causal-discovery estimator / CI test - the SCALAR generator ranking MOVES, the marginalized PROFILE ranking stays STABLE; the Placebo FAILS (method invalid) if either (a) the scalar generator ranking does NOT move across readouts (no confound to disentangle, premise refuted), or (b) the Kendall-tau of the marginalized PROFILE rankings across estimators is not statistically greater than that of the scalar rankings. RECOVERY: numerically verify that B1-B4 collapse exactly to the scalar / "ref." quantities. LOCALIZATION / LEAKAGE probe: injected-distortion recovery (metric iii) plus held-out SCM families (train calibration on Erdos-Renyi, test on scale-free) to rule out overfit attribution. ENSEMBLE-DIVERSITY ABLATION: rerun attribution on a deliberately homogeneous (shared-bias) ensemble - the masking failure must REAPPEAR (injected distortion mis-attributed to readout / consensus error), validating that the diverse ensemble is what restores identifiability. DO-NO-HARM: where one readout is genuinely correct - e.g., DirectLiNGAM on linear non-Gaussian (LU), an identifiable regime - marginalization must NOT wash out that correct verdict; the profile must still rank the faithful generator first.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES (stated clearly). Scalar generator rankings are UNSTABLE across readouts (high rank-variance, low Kendall-tau between estimators). The estimator-marginalized PROFILE yields STABLE rankings (high cross-estimator rank-correlation). The profile LOCALIZES injected distortions with high precision / recall (the fidelity field tracks injected edges). The generator-attributable variance component is CALIBRATED to injected magnitude (monotone, slope bounded away from zero, approaching 1 after calibration). Some generators that look good on the scalar (e.g., strong on synthetic but weak on real, as the paper observes for CoDi and GReaT relative to TabSyn) are EXPOSED as readout-favored rather than causally faithful. Deliverable: an open-source benchmark + metric - the per-claim, estimator-marginalized causal-fidelity profile with variance-components attribution.

## Limitations and Responsible-AI Notes

Scope and nature of contribution
- This is an evaluation / measurement contribution. It introduces no new generative capability and creates no new data-release risk beyond the public datasets and generators it scores.
- All claims are conditional on the readout ensemble (PC/FCI/GES/DirectLiNGAM/NOTEARS/GraN-DAG with Fisher-Z/KCI/RCIT), the SCM coverage (LG/LU/SG/NG), and the specific benchmark datasets; they do not generalize to unseen graphs or domains.

Certificate honesty and over-trust
- The per-claim "certificate" is a conditional audit signal, not a guarantee. Absence of a flag is not proof of causal fidelity; it only means the ensemble did not detect a discrepancy under the tested conditions.
- Counterfactual ground truth is unavailable on real data (Beijing, Sachs); real-data results are necessarily approximate and should not be read as verified causal truth.
- Downstream users must not treat a passing claim as license to deploy synthetic data for high-stakes causal or policy decisions without independent validation.

Attribution honesty and overclaim
- The generator-attributable vs readout-attributable split is itself estimated and can be wrong if the readout ensemble shares a common bias (e.g., shared linearity or faithfulness assumptions); correlated estimator error can be misattributed to the generator.
- Report attribution with uncertainty (bootstrapped confidence intervals); present the profile as a measurement with error bars, not as ground-truth causality.

Privacy
- Synthetic tabular data is frequently used to protect sensitive records (health, finance, census). Higher causal / statistical fidelity can correlate with higher re-identification and membership-inference risk; the fidelity certificate must never be read as a privacy guarantee.
- We flag the fidelity-vs-privacy tension explicitly: optimizing a generator to pass this audit may erode privacy. Pair any fidelity claim with a separate privacy evaluation (e.g., membership-inference, Distance-to-Closest-Record) before release.

Fairness and subgroups
- Graph-level and even claim-level audits can mask subgroup-specific causal distortion. A generator may preserve aggregate causal structure while breaking it within a protected subgroup.
- Where protected or sensitive attributes exist, run subgroup-stratified fidelity and report per-subgroup profiles before using synthetic data for any fairness-sensitive downstream analysis.

Governance, dual-use, and data licensing
- Use only approved / public datasets and respect their licenses and intended-use terms (Beijing air quality, Sachs, bnlearn). Do not extend the benchmark to EHR-like or sensitive-population data without proper consent, governance, and ethics approval.
- Dual-use risk is minimal, but synthetic data of sensitive populations carries governance obligations; document provenance and licensing for every dataset used.

Compute, energy, and reproducibility
- The design (readout ensemble x bootstraps x generators x SCMs) is compute-heavy; report GPU / CPU-hours and energy footprint, and prefer small-scale falsifier-first runs before full sweeps.
- Causal-discovery readouts and bootstraps are stochastic and seed-sensitive; release code, fixed seeds, and configs, and report mean +/- std with confidence intervals rather than single point estimates.
